coredata.dc:title: A First Principles exploration of pristine and p-block doped triangulene carbon dots for Hydrogen evolution reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Kanzariya, Ashvin
Som, Narayan N.
Vadalkar, Shardul
Jana, Sourav Kanti
Saini, L.K.
Jha, Prafulla K.
coredata.dc:description: The global pursuit of clean and sustainable energy sources has driven significant research into the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). In this process, we need catalysts that outperform noble metals, and Triangulene carbon dots (TCDs) are prospective suitors. Therefore, in this study, we explore the potential of pristine and p-block element-doped TCDs as alternative electrocatalysts for HER. Stability is one of the most important virtues for a material to be a good catalyst. We have investigated the cohesive energy and formation energy of doped TCDs, and we observed that N-doped and B-doped TCDs are the most favorable energetically. We observed a lowering of the energy gap with doping, except for Si, leading to an increase in conductivity, which is expected to result in better HER activity. The increased conductivity leads to higher charge transfer between the H atom and TCDs, as validated through Mulliken charge plots. To confirm the enhanced HER activity, we computed the Gibbs free energies, and N-doped TCDs outperformed pristine TCD and B-doped TCD in HER activity, followed by the others, also corroborated by the volcano plot.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triangulene carbon dots
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Density functional theory
Gibbs free energy
Volcano plot

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized geometry of (a) TCD, (b) B-TCD (c) N-TCD (d) P-TCD, (e) S-TCD, (f) Si-TCD.
(a) Bond Length Alteration (BLA) and (b) Angle Alteration (AA) of TCDs.
PDOS and TDOS of TCDs of up-spin molecular orbital.
Minimum energetic configuration of H-adsorbed (a) TCD, (b) B-TCD (c) N-TCD (d) P-TCD, (e) S-TCD, and (f) Si-TCD.
Minimum energetic configuration after 2nd hydrogen adsorption for (a) TCD, (b) B-TCD (c) N-TCD (d) P-TCD, (e) S-TCD, and (f) Si-TCD.
Volcano plot to compare the HER activity of TCDs.
calculated cohesive energy (Ecoh), HOMO- LUMO energies, Fermi energy (EF) and HOMO-LUMO gap (EG) of TCDs.
Calculation of zero-point energy (ZPE) of TCDs.

head.ce:title: A First Principles exploration of pristine and p-block doped triangulene carbon dots for Hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The global pursuit of clean and sustainable energy sources has driven significant research into the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). In this process, we need catalysts that outperform noble metals, and Triangulene carbon dots (TCDs) are prospective suitors. Therefore, in this study, we explore the potential of pristine and p-block element-doped TCDs as alternative electrocatalysts for HER. Stability is one of the most important virtues for a material to be a good catalyst. We have investigated the cohesive energy and formation energy of doped TCDs, and we observed that N-doped and B-doped TCDs are the most favorable energetically. We observed a lowering of the energy gap with doping, except for Si, leading to an increase in conductivity, which is expected to result in better HER activity. The increased conductivity leads to higher charge transfer between the H atom and TCDs, as validated through Mulliken charge plots. To confirm the enhanced HER activity, we computed the Gibbs free energies, and N-doped TCDs outperformed pristine TCD and B-doped TCD in HER activity, followed by the others, also corroborated by the volcano plot.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The energy crisis is primarily caused by rising energy demands, dwindling fossil fuel reserves, geopolitical conflicts, and environmental concerns. It results in economic challenges, social disruptions, and environmental degradation. Solutions involve diversifying energy sources, improving efficiency, and transitioning to sustainable alternatives [1]. The Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER) embodies an interdisciplinary solution that amalgamates chemistry, physics, and engineering to produce clean and versatile hydrogen. Its seamless integration with renewable energy, mitigation of fossil fuel reliance, and provision of a robust energy storage framework culminate in a formidable response to the energy crisis. This multifaceted approach forms a crucial cornerstone in the realization of a more sustainable and resilient energy future [2]. The HER, represented as 2H+ + 2e− → H2, is a two electron steps electrochemical process governed by two major reaction path mechanisms so called the Volmer−Tafel (VT) mechanism and the Volmer−Heyrovsky (VH) mechanism [3,4]. Both pathways share a common initial step involving the adsorption of a primary hydrogen atom (H*) at the catalytic surface (say Volmer hydrogen). In the VT mechanism, the reaction proceeds by the subsequent adsorption of a secondary hydrogen atom on the catalyst surface, followed by the recombination of the adsorbates to form molecular hydrogen (H2) [5]. This mechanism involves two sequential adsorption events of hydrogen atoms and their subsequent coupling to generate H2. Conversely, the VH mechanism facilitates H2 formation through the interaction of a proton (H +) from the solution with the adsorbed hydrogen atom (H*). In this case, the adsorbed H atom reacts with a proton from the electrolyte solution to produce H2 [6]. Catalyst's choice, surface area, and crystal structures are the keys to unlocking efficient hydrogen generation. In acidic conditions, the Hydrogen Binding/adsorption Energy (HBE/ΔEH) is a widely accepted indicator of HER activity [7]. A volcano plot model correlates catalytic activity with the energetics of hydrogen adsorption on metal surfaces [8] . When the Gibbs free energy change for hydrogen adsorption approaches zero, it predicts the highest HER activity [9–10]. This model aids in catalyst selection for efficient and sustainable energy production.

Quantum dots (QDs) have a wide range of applications across various scientific fields, particularly in advancing the HER. As efficient photocatalysts, QDs drive hydrogen production by generating electron-hole pairs under illumination. Additionally, they enhance photon absorption when used as sensitizers in photoelectrochemical cells, boost catalytic efficiency as co-catalysts, and improve charge transfer in electrode modifications. In tandem solar cells, they capture high-energy photons for electricity generation in water electrolysis [11], supporting the HER. This versatility underscores their potential to advance sustainable hydrogen production in clean energy technologies. From this application researcher are focusing on QDs for HER active catalyst. Allangawi and their group prove potential application of the Co@C12, Ni@C12, Cu@C12 and Ni@Al6N6 systems as highly stable, highly conductive, metal free and highly active single atom catalysts (SACs) for the HER using density functional theory (DFT) calculation [12]. Furthermore, Hossain and their collaborators derived catalytic activity for a series of transition metals embedded on N-doped graphene as SACs and found that Co-SACs exhibits highest HER activity [13]. Abdulrahman et al. employed DFT calculation to design late-first row transition metal atoms (Fe-Zn) supported on B12P12 and Al12P12 nano-cage as new HER SACs. They propose the use of Ni@Al12P12, Ni@B12P12 and Co@B12P12 SACs as abundant earth metal, sufficient stable and highly efficient novel electrocatalysts for the HER [14]. Also, Sehrish Sarfaraz and the group simultaneously consider the thermodynamic and kinetic aspects to describe the HER activity of the first-row transition metals (Sc to Zn) doped C24 nanocage as SACs using quantum chemical DFT study. They achieve the free energy
Δ
G H
value for Mn@C24 catalyst is 0.07 eV, which is closed to zero, required for the optimal activity of catalyst for HER [9]. Moreover, Ernest C. Agwamba and their group evaluate fullerene with encapsulated, doped, and decorated TMs (Mo, Fe, Au) systems as efficient electro-catalysts for HER. Their results indicate that AuFeMoC and FeMoc surfaces show the strongest catalytic activity and are promising candidates for hydrogen production in HER [15]. Researchers have used Graphene Quantum Dots and Triangulene Carbon dot (TCD) as a Hazardous gas sensor and remover [16,22]. To our best knowledge TCD, have not be investigated as electrocatalyst for HER activity.

Moreover, experimentalists synthesized Triangulene Carbon Dot (TCD) by Top-Down approach (like laser ablation or electrochemical methods) or Bottom up approach (like chemical synthesis) [17–19], motivates us to investigate the HER activity and provide bench mark for experimental realization. Zhidong Chang, et al. synthesized B-doped TCD and its use as optoelectronic applications [20]. The group of Tao Wang, synthesized nitrogen-doped TCD on both Au(111) and Ag(111) surfaces by H reduction, followed by annealing and tip manipulations [21]. Furthermore, Researchers proves that TCD and impurity doped TCD can be used as a sensing of toxic environmental gases, Hydrogen storage and electrocatalyst for ORR [22,23]. However, HER activity has not been investigated on these doped TCD up to now. This further, motivates us to investigate the HER activity of TCD and doping it with P-block elements like Boron, Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Sulfur, and Silicon to elevates their reactivity by creating specialized surface sites and adjusting electronic properties. Boron introduces Lewis's acid sites, Nitrogen adds Lewis basicity, Phosphorus forms active sites, Sulfur acts as an electron reservoir, and Silicon enhances stability. This synergy intensifies catalyst-reactant interactions, boosting efficiency and selectivity in various reactions. Researchers are increasingly drawn to these cost-effective dopants, making them indispensable for custom-tailored catalysts to address HER activity [24–31]. This is the sole reason, we doped above P- block elements in TCD for investigating HER catalytic activity.

To investigate the structural and electronical properties and catalytic activity of pristine and doped (B, N, P, S and Si) TCD, the calculation were performed using density functional theory (DFT) as implemented in Gaussian09 software [32]. The choice of the hybrid B3LYP functionals with a, 6–31 G and ωB97XD basis set for geometry optimization and energy calculations was motivated by the open-shell nature of triangulene [23]. We describe the results of hybrid B3LYP functionals with a 6–31 G (d,p) with Grimme's DFT-D3(BJ) dispersion correction and BSSE correction using counterpoise method was employed to void the underestimation and to get more accurate results [33,34]. GaussView version 6 [35] was used for structure visualization, Mulliken charges, and energy levels. Multiwfn 3.7 dev [36] generated partial and total density of states. For study of vibrational mode of TCDs, we plotted the IR spectrum using GaussSum software [37]. During the calculation, the convergence criteria are 12 × 10−4 radian, 18 × 10−4 Bohr, 3 × 10−4 Hartree/radian, and 45 × 10−5 Hartree/Bohr for gradients of root-mean-square (rms) displacement, for maximum displacement, for rms force, and for maximum force, respectively.

We evaluated the Cohesive energy of systems for ensuring the stability, which can be mathematically represented as follow [38–40]:
(1)
E coh
=
1 n
(
E system
− x
E C
− y
E H
−
E dopant
)
Here, Ecoh denotes the cohesive energy of the system, while Esystem, EC, EH, and Edopant correspond to the total energies of the system, isolated carbon atom, isolated hydrogen atom, and isolated dopant atom, respectively. x and y represent the total numbers of carbon atoms and hydrogen atoms, respectively. n is the total number of atoms present in systems.

The Fermi level (EF) and Energy gap, the difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), (EG) are given by [16,41]
(2)
E F
=
(
E LUMO
+
E HOMO
2
)
(3)
E G
=
E LUMO
−
E HOMO
where ELUMO and EHOMO are the LUMO energy and HOMO energy, respectively.

HER in acid conditions, follows the below elementary steps: where the Volmer - Tafel mechanism follows Eqs. (4) and 5, and Volmer - Heyrovsky follows Eqs. (4) and 6 [23,42,43].
(4)
Volmer :
*
+
H
+
+
e
−
→
H
*
(5)
Tafel :
H
*
+
H
*

H 2

2
*
(6)
Heyrovsky :
H
*
+
H
+
+
e
−

H 2
+
*
Where *, H +, e- and H* corresponds to surface, proton, electron and the adsorbed H atom of system, respectively. The adsorption energy of H atom was computed using the formula [5]:
(7)
E
ads
H
=
E
system + H

E system

)
Where
E
system + H
denotes total energy of proton (Volmer hydrogen) adsorbed over systems,
E system
represents total energy of system and
E H
2
stands for energy of optimized hydrogen molecule. According to Norskov et al. [44], the hydrogen adsorption (
E
ads
H
) and Gibbs free energy (
Δ

)
is calculated using the equations given below;
(8)
Δ

E

Where
Δ

Z P E
is the difference in zero-point energy between the adsorbed H and a gaseous H2,
Δ
S H
is the change in entropy between adsorbed state and the gas phase, and T is room temperature of 298 .15K. The calculated values of
Δ
E
Z P E
,
T
Δ
S H
are given in Table 2.

Catalytic activity can also be estimated through the value of over potential
( η )
and exchange current density
(
i 0
)
, which calculated using following equations; [8,9,45,46]
(9)
η
=
|
Δ
G H
|
e
(10)
i 0
= − e
K 0
1
1 +
e
|
Δ
G H
|
K B
T
Where e is electron charge, KB is Boltzmann constant (eV/K), T = 298 K and rate constant K0 =1 s-1 site-1 [40,47–49].

Triangulene Carbon Dot (TCD) is a triangular structure with a triplet ground state [23] is found to be ground state. Its possession of an even number of carbon atoms, specifically, 22 arranged in six fused benzene rings. Our findings reveal specific molecular parameters of pristine TCD, such as a CC bond length of 1.42 Å, a bond angle of 120°, consistent with previously reported works [23]. TCD exhibit a linear 0-D structure. Upon doping with B, P, S and Si by replacing the middle C atom in TCD, an enlargement of the bond length with negligible change in bond angle near dopant is observed due to lower electronegativity of B, P, S and Si atoms compared to C atom. Contrarily, in N doped TCD, decrease in bond length is observed, due to higher electronegativity of N atom compared to C atom. The structures of TCD, B-doped TCD (B-TCD), N-doped TCD (N-TCD), P-doped TCD (P-TCD), S-doped TCD (S-TCD) and Si-doped TCD (Si-TCD) remains linear with no distortion before and after the doping due similar atomic radii of dopants compared to the C atom. Fig. 1 presents the optimized geometry of TCD, B-TCD, N-TCD, P-TCD, S-TCD, and Si-TCD.

The Cohesive energy is the amount of energy required to build up a material from isolated atoms, also known as the atomization energy. Cohesive energies (Eq. (1)) are essential in understanding the thermodynamics of chemical reactions, as they provide insights into whether a reaction is exothermic (releases energy) or endothermic (absorbs energy). Positive cohesive energies indicate that a compound is less stable than its constituent elements in their standard states, while negative cohesive energies suggest that a compound is more stable than its constituent elements [50]. These values are tabulated for various systems and are useful in predicting the feasibility and energetics of chemical reactions and in determining the stability of different substances under various conditions. The values of cohesive energies for pristine and doped TCDs are listed in table 1 . Negative energies of all TCDs prove the stability of structures as it's exothermic. The highest negative values are observed for Pristine TCD (−6.92 eV), with an absolute decrement in cohesive energy of 13 % for B-doped TCD, 8 % for N-doped TCD, 23 % for P-doped TCD, 32 % for S-doped TCD, and 23 % for Si-doped TCD compared to pristine TCD. Moreover, we calculate the formation energies of x-doped TCD (x = B, N, P, S, Si) form the constituent considered gas to form doped TCD using the Eq. (11), which is widely used previously [24,51,52].
(11)
E
f o r m
=
E
x − T C D
−
E
T C D
+
μ
C
−
μ
x
Where,
E
x −
T C D
and
E
T C D
are energies of doped system and pristine,
μ C
and
μ x
are chemical potential of carbon atom and dopant, respectively. The calculation of chemical potential as;
μ B
=
E
B
H 3
−
3 2
E
H
2
f o r
B − T C D
,
μ N
=
1 2
E
N 2
f o r
N − T C D
,
μ P
=
E
P
H 3
−
3 2
E
H
2
f o r
P − T C D
,
μ S
=
E
H 2
S
−
E
H
2
f o r
S − T C D
,
μ
S i
=
E
S i
H 4
− 2
E
H 2
f o r
S i − T C D
. Here,
E
H 2
,
E
B
H 3
,
E
N 2
,
E
P
H 3
,
E
H 2
S
a n d
E
S i
H 4
are energies of hydrogen gas, Borane, Nitrogen gas, Phosphine, Hydrogen Sulphide and silane in gas phase. The values of formation energies with zero-point energy correction are −0.57 eV, 0.41 eV, 1.11 eV, 5.11 eV and 2.98 eV respectively for B-TCD, N-TCD, P-TCD, S-TCD and Si-TCD. Negative formation energy indicates the exothermic reaction and positive indicates the endothermic reaction, which is less stable than its pristine TCD. Furthermore, these values highlight that, the formation of B-doped TCD from pristine TCD and borane releases 0.57 eV of energy. In contrast, the formation of N-doped TCD from pristine TCD and N2, P-doped TCD from pristine TCD and phosphine, S-doped TCD from pristine TCD and sulphide, and Si-doped TCD from pristine TCD and silane requires 0.41 eV, 1.11 eV, 5.11 eV, and 2.98 eV, respectively. These results conclude that B-TCD and N-TCD requires less energy to form the structure, whereas the P-TCD, Si-TCD and S-TCD requires large amount of the energy. Therefore, B-TCD and N-TCD are most favourable doping configurations among all others.

We analyse the IR spectra of TCD, to observe the change after doping and stability with positive frequency. TCD is a polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon, which can be characterized by key vibrational modes reflecting its aromatic structure and the aromatic CH stretching vibrations, We observe an appear as sharp peaks in the region of 3000–3100 cm⁻¹ (see Fig. 2 , TCD), indicative of hydrogen atoms bonded to sp² hybridized carbons in the aromatic rings. The aromatic C = C stretching vibrations produce strong peaks in the range of 1450–1680 cm⁻¹, corresponding to the conjugated C = C (1594.4 cm-1), in the structure. The peaks at 752.49 cm-1, 963 cm-1 and, 1231.28 cm-1 related to benzene ring well matched with previous report [18]. Additionally, out-of-plane CH bending vibrations are observed in the 700–900 cm⁻¹ region, which are typical for aromatic compounds with specific substitution patterns. Together, these features provide clear evidence of the molecule's aromaticity and its characteristic triangular geometry. We see the major changes in the region 1450–1600 cm-1 in case of B-TCD, N-TCD, and Si-TCD, replacing carbon atom in centre of TCD with boron (B), nitrogen (N), or silicon (Si) alters the electron density and conjugation within the aromatic system, leading to shifts in the IR absorption bands associated with aromatic C = C stretching (See Fig. 2). Incorporation of boron, being electron-deficient, tends to lower the electron density, slightly shifting the bands to lower wavenumbers (1578.93 cm-1) due to increased bond strength (B-C 1.49 Å). For, N-TCD, we observe shift the bands to lower wavenumbers (1550 cm-1). Silicon, with its larger size and weaker π-interactions, reduces delocalization, typically broadening the peaks and slightly shifting them to lower wavenumbers (1545 cm-1). Phosphorus, with its ability to donate electron density, alters the region 500–1000 cm-1 which associate with benzene ring vibrations, while sulfur, being more polarizable and slightly electron-donating, similarly shift compare to Si dopant and broaden the peaks in region of 1400–1600 cm-1. These changes reflect the influence of the dopants on the aromaticity and vibrational characteristics of benzene ring Moreover, the peaks indicate region of the spectrum where specific bond vibrations occur and absence of negative frequency suggest that system is dynamical stable (see Fig. 2) [53]. Fig. 3 illustrates the variations in bond length alteration (BLA) and angle alteration (AA) for TCDs. A comprehensive summary of the bond lengths and bond angles is provided in the supplementary information, Section 4. The plots distinctly indicate that changes in bond lengths and angles are most pronounced near the dopant site, suggesting that the dopant modifies the electronic distribution within the system.

To gain insight into the electronic properties of TCDs, we plots the partial densities of states (PDOS). The energy gap between the highest occupied level and the lowest unoccupied level is often called the HOMO-LUMO gap, considered as a key determinant of a material's electrical behaviour, influencing whether it acts as an insulator (EG>3 eV), semiconductor (0.1≤EG≤3), or metal (EG=0) [54]. Table 1 and Figure s2(a-f, in supporting information) illustrate the HOMO - LUMO electron density and HOMO-LUMO gap of pristine and doped TCDs. In TCD, B-TCD, N-TCD, P-TCD and Si-TCD, there is splitting of energy states into α-molecular orbitals (MO) and ß-molecular orbitals. However, in S-TCD, there is no splitting of energy levels into α-MO and ß-MO. Pristine TCD shows alpha (up-spin) HOMO at −4.50 eV, beta (downspin) HOMO at −6.19 eV, alpha LUMO at −0.70 eV, beta LUMO at −2.46 eV, with corresponding alpha HOMO-LUMO energy gaps of 3.80 eV and beta HOMO-LUMO gap of 3.73 (listed in Table 1). The alpha (beta) HOMO-LUMO gap of B-TCD, N-TCD, P-TCD, S-TCD and Si-TCD are 1.05 eV (2.01 eV), 2.10 eV (0.89 eV), 2.69 eV (0.82 eV), 1.71 eV, 4.01 eV (2.42 eV), respectively. Notably, the dissimilarity in the HOMO-LUMO values between alpha and beta phases stems from the spatial separation of the two unpaired electrons, contributing differentially to each phase. This outcome aligns with previous research. [18,19,23,55–57].

In Figs. 4 , dashed line corresponds to Fermi level, which is set at zero. Figure depicts that the HOMO-LUMO gap consistently decreases after doping except Si-TCD, which led to an increase in conductivity and is anticipated to enhance HER activity.

To assess HER catalytic activity of the TCD, B-doped TCD, N-doped TCD, P-doped TCD, S-doped TCD, and Si-doped TCD, hydrogen atoms were adsorbed at all possible sites, say on C0, C1, C2, C3, C4 and C5. This configuration is highlighted with different colour in supporting information of figure s3. For further calculation, we consider the minimum energy configuration out of six possible configurations. The minimum energetic configuration of the first hydrogen adsorption (Volmer step) for the aforementioned systems is depicted in Fig. 5 . In this figure, pink colour highlights the adsorbed H atom to different from H atoms of TCD and doped-TCD. With H adsorption, we observed the charges transfer from H atom to the considered system. The value of transfer of charge is about −0.1696 e, - 0.1589 e, - 0.1795 e, - 0.2138 e, - 0.0428 e, and - 0.2044 e in TCD, B-doped TCD, N-doped TCD, P-doped TCD, S-doped TCD, and Si-doped TCD, respectively. This charge transfer implies an interaction between the system and the H atom. We observed the highest charge transfer for P-TCD and Si-TCD, which can be attributed to strong interaction between adsorbent and adsorbate consistent with the adsorption energy values. Furthermore, structure of pristine and doped-TCD is planner before adsorption of H atom. Upon H adsorption, the structure of P-doped TCD, S-doped TCD, and Si-doped TCD changes from planar to bent towards the direction of H adsorption. In P-doped TCD, the C-P-C angle change from 120° to 99.58°, and from 180° to 131.78°, 137.65°, and 128.66°, while the C-P bond length increases from 1.65 Å to 1.80 Å In S-doped TCD, the C-S-C angle change from 120° to 101.35° and from 180° to 132.35°, 140.38°, and 134.56°, with the C-S bond length increases from 1.64 Å to 1.79 Å In Si-doped TCD, the C-Si-C angles change from 120° to 106°, and from 180° to 135.85°, 144.77°, and 135.94°, while the C-Si bond length changes from 1.69 Å to 1.79Å

Change in adsorption energy (ΔEH) and Gibbs free energy (ΔGH) are key determinants for evaluating HER catalytic activity. For optimal HER activity, a Gibbs free energy close to zero and a zero overpotential are desirable [9,12]. We calculated the Gibbs free energy, overpotential and adsorption energy of H adsorbed TCDs using the Eqs. (7) to 9. The calculated values are presented in Table 3. For these calculations, the systems were optimized using the B3LYP/631G(d,p) functional with D3BJ dispersion and counterpoise corrections. The H adsorption energies for TCD, B-doped TCD, N-doped TCD, P-doped TCD, S-doped TCD, and Si-doped TCD were found to be −0.52 eV, −0.76 eV, −0.50 eV, −1.99 eV, −2.67 eV, and −1.47, respectively. The negative adsorption energy values with smaller magnitudes indicate physisorption between the adsorbed H atom and TCDs. Conversely, negative values with larger magnitude signify chemisorption. Thus, in TCD, B-doped TCD and N-doped TCD, H is weakly adsorbed, which is desirable for HER activity. In contrast, in P-doped TCD, S-doped TCD and Si-doped TCD, the H atom attaches strongly to the system, hindering the H2 evolution and rendering them for HER.

For calculation of Gibbs free energy, we required the values of
Δ
E
Z P E
− T
Δ
S H
. Table 2 highlights the calculation of these parameters. ΔEZPE represents the difference between the zero-point energy of adsorbed H atom and half of zero-point energy of H2 molecule in the gas phase (
Δ
E
Z P E
= Z P
E
H *
−
1 2
Z P
E
H 2
)
. The zero-point energy (ZPE) of adsorbed H atom is equivalent to the vibrational frequency correspond to adsorbed H atom, while ZPE of H2 is equal to the vibration frequency of H2. These values are obtained from frequency calculations of system. Similarly, ΔSH represents the difference between the entropy of adsorbed H atom (SH*) to half of entropy of H2 molecule in gas phase
(
Δ
S H
=
S
H *
−
1 2
S
H 2
)
. The entropy of the adsorbed H atom (SH*) is calculated as the difference between the total entropy of the H-adsorbed system and the total entropy of the system without the adsorbed H atom (
S
H *
=
S
H − s y s t e m
−
S
s y s t e m
)
listed in Table 2.

After calculating the values of ΔEZPE-TΔSH, the Gibbs free energy was determined using Eq. (8). The Gibbs free energy values for the TCDs are presented in Table 3 . The minimum value was found in N-doped TCD (−0.19 eV), while the maximum value was found in S-doped TCD (−2.43 eV). The ΔGH values for TCD (−0.26 eV), B-doped TCD (−0.51 eV), and N-doped TCD (−0.19 eV) are close to zero, indicating that these systems are suitable for HER catalysis. In contrast, the ΔGH values for P-doped TCD (−1.74 eV), S-doped TCD (−2.43 eV), and Si-doped TCD (−1.22 eV) are significantly far from zero, suggesting that these systems are unsuitable for HER.

The Volmer step, representing the minimal energetic configuration of the first hydrogen adsorption, is an intermediate stage in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). To complete the reaction and form a stable H2 molecule, a second hydrogen atom must adsorb onto the system. The formation of the H2 molecule can occur via the Volmer-Tafel or Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanisms, which are described in detail in the Introduction section. In this study, pristine TCD, boron-doped TCD, and nitrogen-doped TCD were observed to evolve H2 molecules through the Volmer-Heyrovsky pathway, as shown in Fig. 6 . In this figure pink colour highlights the adsorbed H atoms. The calculated H2 binding energies for pristine, B-doped, and N-doped TCD were −0.04 eV, −0.07 eV, and −0.06 eV, respectively. These values indicate that the system can be considered a potential catalyst for HER.

In summary, this study emphasizes the promising potential of pristine and p-block element (B, N, Si, P, and S) doped Triangulene Carbon Dots (TCDs) as alternative electrocatalysts for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). Our findings indicate that N-doped and B-doped TCDs are the most energetically favorable candidates, exhibiting superior stability, as confirmed by cohesive energy and formation energy calculations. Doping, with the exception of silicon, led to a reduction in the energy gap, enhancing conductivity and, consequently, HER activity. This improved conductivity facilitated better charge transfer between hydrogen atoms and TCDs, as demonstrated by Mulliken charge plots. The enhanced HER performance was further validated by Gibbs free energy (ΔGH) calculations, where N-doped TCDs exhibited the highest HER activity, followed by pristine and B-doped TCDs, as corroborated by the volcano plot.

The structural stability of the doped TCDs was confirmed by negative cohesive energies and the absence of imaginary frequencies in infrared spectra. Moreover, density of states (DOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) analyses confirmed the semiconducting nature of these materials, with dopants contributing significantly to the energy levels. The ΔGH values and overpotentials for N-doped TCD, pristine TCD, and B-doped TCD were −0.19 eV (190 mV), −0.26 eV (260 mV), and −0.51 eV (510 mV), respectively, indicating their potential as effective electrocatalysts for HER. These results suggest that N-doped TCDs outperform the other materials tested and are a promising candidate for efficient hydrogen evolution.

Ashvin Kanzariya: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Narayan N. Som: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Shardul Vadalkar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Sourav Kanti Jana: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. L.K. Saini: Supervision, Resources. Prafulla K. Jha: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: AK is grateful to the SVNIT, Surat, for the institute research fellowship (FIR- D22PH006).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.mcat.2024.114766.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SUPPORTING INFORMATION SUMMARY Section 1 includes the calculation of hydrogen adsorption energies of Triangulene Carbon Dot (TCDs) with different basis sets. Section 2 highlights the ESP maps of TCDs. Section 3 contains the values of bond lengths and angles in TCDs. HOMO-LUMO diagrams of pristine and doped TCD are included in section 4. Finally, in section 5 we plotted the Mulliken charge of individual atom inside the TCDs.
